title: AcyclicityUnderIntervention

tex_statement.html: If a CDMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$ is acyclic then also $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is acyclic and a topological order for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is also one for $G$. Any topological order of $G$ can be extended to one for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$, e.g.\ by putting all the $I_w$ nodes first in the ordering.

% Claim proof file for row claim_3_13 -- "AcyclicityUnderIntervention".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solving_current_row.py at row start. A
% manager agent dedicated to writing the TeX proof fills in the body below.
% The proof must:
%   - Stay close to the lecture notes' proof if one exists (always search
%     the LN first for a `\begin{proof}` block immediately following the
%     claim; copy / lightly edit when present).
%   - Otherwise use the LN paradigm and cite prior claims by their `ref`.
%   - Be self-contained enough that a mathematician can follow it without
%     re-reading the LN.
%   - Have no `sorry`, `omitted`, or "obvious" shortcuts.
%
% Compiles standalone via the `subfiles` package.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_13 (proof, with statement restated for readability)
% title: AcyclicityUnderIntervention
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_13}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_13}
%
% Cross-references: see claim_<ref>_statement_<title>.tex in this folder
% for the corresponding statement.

% --- statement (restated) ---------------------------------------------------
\begin{Rem}[AcyclicityUnderIntervention]
    If a CDMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$ is acyclic then also $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is acyclic and a topological order for
    $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is also one for $G$.
    Any topological order of $G$ can be extended to one for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$, e.g.\
    by putting all the $I_w$ nodes first in the ordering.
\end{Rem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
% Proof constructed from scratch in the LN paradigm: the LN's \Rem at
% lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex:821--829 contains no \begin{proof}
% block (the next claimmark, claim_3_14, follows immediately at line 831).
% The three parts mirror the three theorems in the companion Lean file
% AcyclicityUnderInterventionNodes.lean (parts A / B / C correspond to
% isAcyclic_..., ..._restrict, ..._extend respectively); the named order in
% Part C mirrors \refrow{claim_3_6}'s standalone splitOrder relation.
\begin{proof}
The whole argument rests on a single edge-set observation: by \refrow{def_3_13} item iii,
\[
  E_{\doit(I_W)}
  \;=\;
  E \;\dcup\; \lC I_w \tuh w \,\middle|\, w \in W \sm J \rC,
\]
i.e.\ every edge of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is either an original edge of $G$ or a fresh edge $I_w \tuh w$ for some $w \in W \sm J$. In particular, the \refrow{def_3_1} invariant $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$ together with the fresh-edge target $w \in W \sm J \ins V$ (using the precondition $W \ins J \cup V$ to rule out $w \in W \sm J$ being outside $G$, leaving $w \in V$) gives that \emph{every} edge of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ has its target in $V$:
\[
  E_{\doit(I_W)} \;\ins\; \lp J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)} \rp \times V.
\]
By contrast, the \emph{source} of an edge can be either an original node $v' \in J \cup V$ (first disjunct, $E$-piece) or a fresh intervention node $I_w$ (second disjunct). This source--target asymmetry is the key observation driving all three parts below.

\medskip\noindent\emph{Part A (acyclicity).}
Suppose, for contradiction, that $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is not acyclic. By \refrow{def_3_6} there is a node $v$ of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ admitting a non-trivial directed walk to itself:
\[
  v = v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n = v
  \qquad \text{in } G_{\doit(I_W)}, \quad n \ge 1,
\]
i.e.\ $(v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$ for every $k = 0, \dots, n-1$ (\refrow{def_3_4}).

In a directed cycle every vertex is the target of some edge: for each $k \in \lC 1, \dots, n \rC$ the step $(v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$ has $v_k$ as its target, and the closure $v_0 = v_n$ then makes $v_0 = v_n$ a target as well. By the edge-set observation above, every target of an edge of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ lies in $V$. Hence $v_k \in V$ for all $k = 0, \dots, n$; in particular no $v_k$ is one of the fresh $I_w$ intervention nodes.

We now show each step is an original $E$-edge. Fix $k$ and consider $(v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$. By \refrow{def_3_13} item iii it is either an original edge $(v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E$, or a fresh edge $I_w \tuh w$ for some $w \in W \sm J$. The fresh-edge case is impossible: its source is $I_w$, a fresh intervention node, but we just showed $v_{k-1} \in V$ is original. So $(v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E$.

The same vertex sequence
\[
  v = v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n = v
  \qquad \text{in } G
\]
is therefore a non-trivial directed walk in $G$ (\refrow{def_3_4}). Since $v_0 \in V$, in particular $v_0$ is a node of $G$ (\refrow{def_3_2}), so $v_0$ admits a non-trivial directed walk to itself in $G$, contradicting acyclicity of $G$ (\refrow{def_3_6}).

\medskip\noindent\emph{Part B (restriction direction).}
Assume $W \ins J \cup V$ and that $<'$ is a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ (\refrow{def_3_8}). We claim that the restriction
\[
  v < w \;:\iff\; v <' w \qquad (v, w \in J \cup V)
\]
is a topological order of $G$. Note that the canonical embedding $J \cup V \hookrightarrow J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ is the identity: by \refrow{def_3_13} items i.--ii., $J_{\doit(I_W)} = J \dcup \lC I_w \mid w \in W \sm J \rC$ and $V_{\doit(I_W)} = V$, so $J \ins J_{\doit(I_W)}$ and $V = V_{\doit(I_W)}$, giving $J \cup V \ins J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$. Hence every $v \in G$ is also a node of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ (\refrow{def_3_2}). We verify the four conditions of \refrow{def_3_8} in turn.

\smallskip
\noindent\emph{Irreflexivity on $G$.} For $v \in G = J \cup V$, the embedding gives $v \in G_{\doit(I_W)}$, so $\neg(v <' v)$ by irreflexivity of $<'$. By definition of $<$, this is $\neg(v < v)$. \checkmark

\smallskip
\noindent\emph{Transitivity on $G$.} For $v, w, x \in G$, all three lie in $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ by the embedding. If $v < w$ and $w < x$, unfolding the definition gives $v <' w$ and $w <' x$, hence $v <' x$ by transitivity of $<'$, i.e.\ $v < x$. \checkmark

\smallskip
\noindent\emph{Trichotomy on $G$.} For $v, w \in G$, the embedding gives $v, w \in G_{\doit(I_W)}$, and trichotomy of $<'$ yields $v <' w \lor v = w \lor w <' v$, equivalently $v < w \lor v = w \lor w < v$. \checkmark

\smallskip
\noindent\emph{Parents precede children.} Suppose $v \in \Pa^G(w)$. By \refrow{def_3_5} this means $v \in G$ together with $(v, w) \in E$. The first disjunct of \refrow{def_3_13} item iii embeds $E$ into $E_{\doit(I_W)}$, so $(v, w) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$. Combined with $v \in G_{\doit(I_W)}$ (from the embedding), \refrow{def_3_5} gives $v \in \Pa^{G_{\doit(I_W)}}(w)$. The topological-order condition for $<'$ on $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ then yields $v <' w$, i.e.\ $v < w$. \checkmark

Combining the four checks, $<$ is a topological order of $G$ in the sense of \refrow{def_3_8}.

\medskip\noindent\emph{Part C (extension direction with explicit recipe).}
Conversely, assume $W \ins J \cup V$ and that $<$ is a topological order of $G$. We construct a topological order $<'$ of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ by the LN's recipe: put all $I_w$ nodes first, ordered among themselves by the underlying $w \in W \sm J$ values, and inherit $<$ on the original nodes. Explicitly, on the carrier
\[
  J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}
  \;=\; (J \cup V) \,\dcup\, \lC I_w \,\middle|\, w \in W \sm J \rC
\]
(by \refrow{def_3_13} items i.--ii.\ and the precondition $W \ins J \cup V$), define
\[
  a <' b \;:\iff\;
  \begin{cases}
    a < b & \text{if } a, b \in J \cup V \text{ (both original),} \\
    w_1 < w_2 & \text{if } a = I_{w_1}, \ b = I_{w_2} \text{ (both fresh),} \\
    \mathrm{true} & \text{if } a = I_w \text{ (fresh), } b \in J \cup V \text{ (original),} \\
    \mathrm{false} & \text{if } a \in J \cup V \text{ (original), } b = I_w \text{ (fresh).}
  \end{cases}
\]
The four cases are mutually exclusive and exhaustive because $J \cup V$ and $\lC I_w \mid w \in W \sm J \rC$ are disjoint (the second component of the disjoint union in $J_{\doit(I_W)}$, \refrow{def_3_13} item i.). We verify the four conditions of \refrow{def_3_8} by case analysis on which quadrant each vertex lies in.

\smallskip
\noindent\emph{Irreflexivity.} Let $a \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$.
\begin{itemize}
  \item $a \in J \cup V$ (original): $a <' a$ unfolds to $a < a$, false by irreflexivity of $<$ on $G$.
  \item $a = I_w$ (fresh): $a <' a$ unfolds to $w < w$, false by irreflexivity of $<$ on $G$ (using $w \in W \sm J \ins V \ins G$ from the precondition $W \ins J \cup V$).
\end{itemize}
In neither case does $a <' a$ hold. \checkmark

\smallskip
\noindent\emph{Transitivity.} Let $a, b, c \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ with $a <' b$ and $b <' c$; we show $a <' c$. We label each vertex by its quadrant -- $O$ for original ($\in J \cup V$) or $F$ for fresh ($= I_w$ for some $w \in W \sm J$) -- and case-split on the triple $(a, b, c) \in \lC O, F \rC^3$. The key constraint is that any cross step $O \to F$ has truth value $\mathrm{false}$ (by definition of $<'$), so any case in which either premise $a <' b$ or $b <' c$ is of the form $O \to F$ is vacuous. The eight cases split as follows.
\begin{itemize}
  \item $OOO$: $a <' b$ and $b <' c$ unfold to $a < b$ and $b < c$ in $G$; transitivity of $<$ gives $a < c$, i.e.\ $a <' c$. \checkmark
  \item $FFF$: write $a = I_u, b = I_v, c = I_w$ with $u, v, w \in W \sm J \ins G$ (using the precondition $W \ins J \cup V$). The premises unfold to $u < v$ and $v < w$ in $G$; transitivity of $<$ gives $u < w$, i.e.\ $a <' c$. \checkmark
  \item $FFO$ ($a = I_u, b = I_v, c \in J \cup V$): the first premise unfolds to $u < v$; the second premise is the cross slot $F \to O = \mathrm{true}$. The conclusion $a <' c$ is again the cross slot $F \to O = \mathrm{true}$. \checkmark
  \item $FOO$ ($a = I_u, b, c \in J \cup V$): the first premise is the cross slot $F \to O = \mathrm{true}$; the second premise unfolds to $b < c$. The conclusion $a <' c$ is again the cross slot $F \to O = \mathrm{true}$. \checkmark
  \item $OOF, OFO, OFF, FOF$: each of these has at least one premise of the form $O \to F$, which is $\mathrm{false}$. The hypothesis is vacuous and the implication holds trivially. \checkmark
\end{itemize}
In every non-vacuous case the conclusion $a <' c$ holds. \checkmark

\smallskip
\noindent\emph{Trichotomy.} Let $a, b \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$.
\begin{itemize}
  \item Both original ($a, b \in J \cup V$): trichotomy of $<$ on $G$ gives $a < b \lor a = b \lor b < a$, which unfolds to $a <' b \lor a = b \lor b <' a$.
  \item Both fresh ($a = I_{w_1}, b = I_{w_2}$): trichotomy of $<$ on $G$ at $w_1, w_2 \in W \sm J \ins G$ gives $w_1 < w_2 \lor w_1 = w_2 \lor w_2 < w_1$. The middle disjunct $w_1 = w_2$ gives $I_{w_1} = I_{w_2}$, and the other two unfold to $a <' b$ and $b <' a$.
  \item Cross ($a = I_w$ fresh, $b \in J \cup V$ original): the cross slot $a <' b$ is $\mathrm{true}$, so the first disjunct of trichotomy holds.
  \item Cross ($a \in J \cup V$ original, $b = I_w$ fresh): symmetrically, $b <' a$ is $\mathrm{true}$, so the third disjunct holds.
\end{itemize}
In every case at least one of $a <' b$, $a = b$, $b <' a$ holds. \checkmark

\smallskip
\noindent\emph{Parents precede children.} Suppose $a \in \Pa^{G_{\doit(I_W)}}(b)$, i.e.\ $a \in G_{\doit(I_W)}$ and $(a, b) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$ (\refrow{def_3_5}). By \refrow{def_3_13} item iii, either $(a, b) \in E$ (an original edge) or $(a, b) = (I_w, w)$ for some $w \in W \sm J$ (a fresh edge).
\begin{itemize}
  \item Original edge $(a, b) \in E$: both endpoints lie in $J \cup V$ (\refrow{def_3_1}), and the source observation $a \in G$ (\refrow{def_3_5}) together with the edge $(a, b) \in E$ yields $a \in \Pa^G(b)$. The topological-order condition for $<$ on $G$ gives $a < b$, which by definition is $a <' b$.
  \item Fresh edge $(a, b) = (I_w, w)$: the source is fresh and the target $w \in W \sm J \ins V \ins J \cup V$ is original. So $a <' b$ unfolds to the cross slot $\mathrm{true}$ and holds directly.
\end{itemize}
In both cases $a <' b$. \checkmark

Combining the four checks, $<'$ is a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ in the sense of \refrow{def_3_8}, and by construction it extends $<$ in the sense that the restriction $a <' b \iff a < b$ holds for all $a, b \in J \cup V$ (first quadrant of the case split).
\end{proof}

\end{document}
tex_proof.html:
Remark — AcyclicityUnderIntervention

If a CDMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$ is acyclic then also $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is acyclic and a topological order for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is also one for $G$. Any topological order of $G$ can be extended to one for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$, e.g.\ by putting all the $I_w$ nodes first in the ordering.

Proof.

The whole argument rests on a single edge-set observation: by def_3_13 item iii, \[ E_{\doit(I_W)} \;=\; E \;\dcup\; \lC I_w \tuh w \,\middle|\, w \in W \sm J \rC, \] i.e.\ every edge of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is either an original edge of $G$ or a fresh edge $I_w \tuh w$ for some $w \in W \sm J$. In particular, the def_3_1 invariant $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$ together with the fresh-edge target $w \in W \sm J \ins V$ (using the precondition $W \ins J \cup V$ to rule out $w \in W \sm J$ being outside $G$, leaving $w \in V$) gives that every edge of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ has its target in $V$: \[ E_{\doit(I_W)} \;\ins\; \lp J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)} \rp \times V. \] By contrast, the source of an edge can be either an original node $v' \in J \cup V$ (first disjunct, $E$-piece) or a fresh intervention node $I_w$ (second disjunct). This source–target asymmetry is the key observation driving all three parts below.

\medskip\noindentPart A (acyclicity). Suppose, for contradiction, that $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is not acyclic. By def_3_6 there is a node $v$ of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ admitting a non-trivial directed walk to itself: \[ v = v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n = v \qquad \text{in } G_{\doit(I_W)}, \quad n \ge 1, \] i.e.\ $(v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$ for every $k = 0, \dots, n-1$ (def_3_4).

In a directed cycle every vertex is the target of some edge: for each $k \in \lC 1, \dots, n \rC$ the step $(v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$ has $v_k$ as its target, and the closure $v_0 = v_n$ then makes $v_0 = v_n$ a target as well. By the edge-set observation above, every target of an edge of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ lies in $V$. Hence $v_k \in V$ for all $k = 0, \dots, n$; in particular no $v_k$ is one of the fresh $I_w$ intervention nodes.

We now show each step is an original $E$-edge. Fix $k$ and consider $(v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$. By def_3_13 item iii it is either an original edge $(v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E$, or a fresh edge $I_w \tuh w$ for some $w \in W \sm J$. The fresh-edge case is impossible: its source is $I_w$, a fresh intervention node, but we just showed $v_{k-1} \in V$ is original. So $(v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E$.

The same vertex sequence \[ v = v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n = v \qquad \text{in } G \] is therefore a non-trivial directed walk in $G$ (def_3_4). Since $v_0 \in V$, in particular $v_0$ is a node of $G$ (def_3_2), so $v_0$ admits a non-trivial directed walk to itself in $G$, contradicting acyclicity of $G$ (def_3_6).

\medskip\noindentPart B (restriction direction). Assume $W \ins J \cup V$ and that $<'$ is a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ (def_3_8). We claim that the restriction \[ v < w \;:\iff\; v <' w \qquad (v, w \in J \cup V) \] is a topological order of $G$. Note that the canonical embedding $J \cup V \hookrightarrow J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ is the identity: by def_3_13 items i.–ii., $J_{\doit(I_W)} = J \dcup \lC I_w \mid w \in W \sm J \rC$ and $V_{\doit(I_W)} = V$, so $J \ins J_{\doit(I_W)}$ and $V = V_{\doit(I_W)}$, giving $J \cup V \ins J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$. Hence every $v \in G$ is also a node of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ (def_3_2). We verify the four conditions of def_3_8 in turn.

\smallskip \noindentIrreflexivity on $G$. For $v \in G = J \cup V$, the embedding gives $v \in G_{\doit(I_W)}$, so $\neg(v <' v)$ by irreflexivity of $<'$. By definition of $<$, this is $\neg(v < v)$. \checkmark

\smallskip \noindentTransitivity on $G$. For $v, w, x \in G$, all three lie in $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ by the embedding. If $v < w$ and $w < x$, unfolding the definition gives $v <' w$ and $w <' x$, hence $v <' x$ by transitivity of $<'$, i.e.\ $v < x$. \checkmark

\smallskip \noindentTrichotomy on $G$. For $v, w \in G$, the embedding gives $v, w \in G_{\doit(I_W)}$, and trichotomy of $<'$ yields $v <' w \lor v = w \lor w <' v$, equivalently $v < w \lor v = w \lor w < v$. \checkmark

\smallskip \noindentParents precede children. Suppose $v \in \Pa^G(w)$. By def_3_5 this means $v \in G$ together with $(v, w) \in E$. The first disjunct of def_3_13 item iii embeds $E$ into $E_{\doit(I_W)}$, so $(v, w) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$. Combined with $v \in G_{\doit(I_W)}$ (from the embedding), def_3_5 gives $v \in \Pa^{G_{\doit(I_W)}}(w)$. The topological-order condition for $<'$ on $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ then yields $v <' w$, i.e.\ $v < w$. \checkmark

Combining the four checks, $<$ is a topological order of $G$ in the sense of def_3_8.

\medskip\noindentPart C (extension direction with explicit recipe). Conversely, assume $W \ins J \cup V$ and that $<$ is a topological order of $G$. We construct a topological order $<'$ of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ by the LN's recipe: put all $I_w$ nodes first, ordered among themselves by the underlying $w \in W \sm J$ values, and inherit $<$ on the original nodes. Explicitly, on the carrier \[ J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)} \;=\; (J \cup V) \,\dcup\, \lC I_w \,\middle|\, w \in W \sm J \rC \] (by def_3_13 items i.–ii.\ and the precondition $W \ins J \cup V$), define \[ a <' b \;:\iff\; \begin{cases} a < b & \text{if } a, b \in J \cup V \text{ (both original),} \\ w_1 < w_2 & \text{if } a = I_{w_1}, \ b = I_{w_2} \text{ (both fresh),} \\ \mathrm{true} & \text{if } a = I_w \text{ (fresh), } b \in J \cup V \text{ (original),} \\ \mathrm{false} & \text{if } a \in J \cup V \text{ (original), } b = I_w \text{ (fresh).} \end{cases} \] The four cases are mutually exclusive and exhaustive because $J \cup V$ and $\lC I_w \mid w \in W \sm J \rC$ are disjoint (the second component of the disjoint union in $J_{\doit(I_W)}$, def_3_13 item i.). We verify the four conditions of def_3_8 by case analysis on which quadrant each vertex lies in.

\smallskip \noindentIrreflexivity. Let $a \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$.

  • $a \in J \cup V$ (original): $a <' a$ unfolds to $a < a$, false by irreflexivity of $<$ on $G$.
  • $a = I_w$ (fresh): $a <' a$ unfolds to $w < w$, false by irreflexivity of $<$ on $G$ (using $w \in W \sm J \ins V \ins G$ from the precondition $W \ins J \cup V$).

In neither case does $a <' a$ hold. \checkmark

\smallskip \noindentTransitivity. Let $a, b, c \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ with $a <' b$ and $b <' c$; we show $a <' c$. We label each vertex by its quadrant – $O$ for original ($\in J \cup V$) or $F$ for fresh ($= I_w$ for some $w \in W \sm J$) – and case-split on the triple $(a, b, c) \in \lC O, F \rC^3$. The key constraint is that any cross step $O \to F$ has truth value $\mathrm{false}$ (by definition of $<'$), so any case in which either premise $a <' b$ or $b <' c$ is of the form $O \to F$ is vacuous. The eight cases split as follows.

  • $OOO$: $a <' b$ and $b <' c$ unfold to $a < b$ and $b < c$ in $G$; transitivity of $<$ gives $a < c$, i.e.\ $a <' c$. \checkmark
  • $FFF$: write $a = I_u, b = I_v, c = I_w$ with $u, v, w \in W \sm J \ins G$ (using the precondition $W \ins J \cup V$). The premises unfold to $u < v$ and $v < w$ in $G$; transitivity of $<$ gives $u < w$, i.e.\ $a <' c$. \checkmark
  • $FFO$ ($a = I_u, b = I_v, c \in J \cup V$): the first premise unfolds to $u < v$; the second premise is the cross slot $F \to O = \mathrm{true}$. The conclusion $a <' c$ is again the cross slot $F \to O = \mathrm{true}$. \checkmark
  • $FOO$ ($a = I_u, b, c \in J \cup V$): the first premise is the cross slot $F \to O = \mathrm{true}$; the second premise unfolds to $b < c$. The conclusion $a <' c$ is again the cross slot $F \to O = \mathrm{true}$. \checkmark
  • $OOF, OFO, OFF, FOF$: each of these has at least one premise of the form $O \to F$, which is $\mathrm{false}$. The hypothesis is vacuous and the implication holds trivially. \checkmark

In every non-vacuous case the conclusion $a <' c$ holds. \checkmark

\smallskip \noindentTrichotomy. Let $a, b \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$.

  • Both original ($a, b \in J \cup V$): trichotomy of $<$ on $G$ gives $a < b \lor a = b \lor b < a$, which unfolds to $a <' b \lor a = b \lor b <' a$.
  • Both fresh ($a = I_{w_1}, b = I_{w_2}$): trichotomy of $<$ on $G$ at $w_1, w_2 \in W \sm J \ins G$ gives $w_1 < w_2 \lor w_1 = w_2 \lor w_2 < w_1$. The middle disjunct $w_1 = w_2$ gives $I_{w_1} = I_{w_2}$, and the other two unfold to $a <' b$ and $b <' a$.
  • Cross ($a = I_w$ fresh, $b \in J \cup V$ original): the cross slot $a <' b$ is $\mathrm{true}$, so the first disjunct of trichotomy holds.
  • Cross ($a \in J \cup V$ original, $b = I_w$ fresh): symmetrically, $b <' a$ is $\mathrm{true}$, so the third disjunct holds.

In every case at least one of $a <' b$, $a = b$, $b <' a$ holds. \checkmark

\smallskip \noindentParents precede children. Suppose $a \in \Pa^{G_{\doit(I_W)}}(b)$, i.e.\ $a \in G_{\doit(I_W)}$ and $(a, b) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$ (def_3_5). By def_3_13 item iii, either $(a, b) \in E$ (an original edge) or $(a, b) = (I_w, w)$ for some $w \in W \sm J$ (a fresh edge).

  • Original edge $(a, b) \in E$: both endpoints lie in $J \cup V$ (def_3_1), and the source observation $a \in G$ (def_3_5) together with the edge $(a, b) \in E$ yields $a \in \Pa^G(b)$. The topological-order condition for $<$ on $G$ gives $a < b$, which by definition is $a <' b$.
  • Fresh edge $(a, b) = (I_w, w)$: the source is fresh and the target $w \in W \sm J \ins V \ins J \cup V$ is original. So $a <' b$ unfolds to the cross slot $\mathrm{true}$ and holds directly.

In both cases $a <' b$. \checkmark

Combining the four checks, $<'$ is a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ in the sense of def_3_8, and by construction it extends $<$ in the sense that the restriction $a <' b \iff a < b$ holds for all $a, b \in J \cup V$ (first quadrant of the case split).

lean.name: isAcyclic_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes
lean.code:
/-- claim_3_13 part A: if `G` is acyclic and `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`, then
`G.extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes W hW` is acyclic. Mirrors the
acyclicity half of the `\Rem` immediately after def_3_13 in
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (lines 821 -- 829).

The mathematical content is that every edge of the extended graph
has target `Sum.inl _` (`mem_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_E`:
either it is a `Sum.inl × Sum.inl`-lift of some `(v', w') ∈ G.E`, or
it is a fresh `Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩ → Sum.inl w`, so the target is
`Sum.inl _` in both cases), so a directed cycle cannot pass through
a `Sum.inr` vertex -- there is no edge into a `Sum.inr` vertex. The
cycle therefore lives entirely in `Sum.inl`-lifted edges and
projects to a directed cycle in `G` of the same length,
contradicting `G.IsAcyclic`. See the per-theorem comment block above
for the walk-lifting plan; the precondition `hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`
appears for *typing* reasons only (it is the well-typedness
condition of `extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes`), not as a
mathematical hypothesis of acyclicity preservation.

`G` and `W` are implicit because they are unifiable from `hW` and
from the conclusion; `hW` is explicit because
`extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes` requires it explicitly at every
call site; `h : G.IsAcyclic` is the mathematical hypothesis under
transport. Naming follows Mathlib's `<conclusion>_<construction>`
convention, consistent with `isAcyclic_hardInterventionOn`
(claim_3_3 part A) and `isAcyclic_nodeSplittingOn` (claim_3_6 part
B). -/
theorem isAcyclic_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes
    {G : CDMG α} {W : Set α} (hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) (h : G.IsAcyclic) :
    (G.extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes W hW).IsAcyclic := by

lean.name: extensionOrder
lean.code:
/-- The *extension order* `extensionOrder r` on the carrier `α ⊕ ↑S`,
induced by a base relation `r : α → α → Prop` and a parameter
set `S : Set α`. This is the LN's "putting all `I_w` nodes first"
recipe from claim_3_13: every `Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩` precedes every
`Sum.inl v`, and same-side pairs inherit `r` from the underlying
`α`-values. The four cases of the pattern match correspond to the
four `(Sum.inl/inr, Sum.inl/inr)` corner pairs; see the design
block above and the file-level docstring.

`S` is implicit because it is recovered from the relation's domain
type `α ⊕ ↑S` at the call site; in
`isTopologicalOrder_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_extend` we
spell it `extensionOrder (S := W \ G.J) r` to make the choice
explicit. `noncomputable` because we do not assume any decidability
on `r`; downstream uses are all `Prop`-valued so the classical
choice has no observable cost. Used by
`isTopologicalOrder_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_extend`
(this file) and quoted by name in claim_3_14 / claim_3_15
(commutation) and the iSCM / do-calculus chapters that recurse
along a topological order of an extended CDMG. -/
noncomputable def extensionOrder {S : Set α} (r : α → α → Prop) :
    (α ⊕ ↑S) → (α ⊕ ↑S) → Prop
  | Sum.inl v₁, Sum.inl v₂ => r v₁ v₂
  | Sum.inr ⟨w₁, _⟩, Sum.inr ⟨w₂, _⟩ => r w₁ w₂
  | Sum.inr _, Sum.inl _ => True
  | Sum.inl _, Sum.inr _ => False

lean.name: isTopologicalOrder_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_extend
lean.code:
/-- claim_3_13 part C: if `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` and `r` is a topological
order of `G`, then `extensionOrder (S := W \ G.J) r` is a topological
order of `G.extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes W hW`. Mirrors the
constructive extension direction of the `\Rem` immediately after
def_3_13 in `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (lines 821 --
829), with the LN's "put all the `I_w` nodes first" recipe encoded
as the four-case `extensionOrder` pattern match.

The `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` precondition is structurally required by
`extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes` itself (def_3_13) -- the fresh
edge `Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩ → Sum.inl w` needs its target `Sum.inl w` to
live in the extended graph's `V`-set, which is true only when
`w ∈ G.V` for `w ∈ W \ G.J`. This is not a topological-order
condition; it is the def_3_13 condition transported.

See `isTopologicalOrder_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_restrict`
below for the inverse-direction companion (a topological order of
the extended graph restricts to one of `G`),
`isAcyclic_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes` above for the
acyclicity half, and the file-level docstring for the rationale
behind splitting the LN's single `\Rem` into three theorems plus
one named construction. -/
theorem isTopologicalOrder_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_extend
    {G : CDMG α} {W : Set α} (hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V)
    {r : α → α → Prop} (hr : G.IsTopologicalOrder r) :
    (G.extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes W hW).IsTopologicalOrder
      (extensionOrder (S := W \ G.J) r) := by

lean.name: isTopologicalOrder_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_restrict
lean.code:
/-- claim_3_13 part B: if `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` and `r` is a topological
order of `G.extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes W hW`, then the
restriction `fun v w => r (Sum.inl v) (Sum.inl w)` is a topological
order of `G`. Mirrors the restriction direction of the `\Rem`
immediately after def_3_13 in
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (lines 821 -- 829, first
sentence second clause: "a topological order for `G_{\doit(I_W)}` is
also one for `G`").

The `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` precondition is structurally required by
`extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes` itself (def_3_13), not by the
restriction direction per se; see
`isTopologicalOrder_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_extend` for
the matching extension direction and the file-level docstring for
the rationale behind splitting the LN's single `\Rem` into three
theorems plus one named construction.

Inverse-direction companion to the extension theorem: field-by-field
transport using the fact that `Sum.inl` embedding takes `G`'s nodes
into the extended graph's nodes
(`extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_J` /
`extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_V` make this precise) and
`Sum.inl`-images of `G.E` are edges of the extended graph
(`mem_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_E` first disjunct). -/
theorem isTopologicalOrder_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_restrict
    {G : CDMG α} {W : Set α} (hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V)
    {r : (α ⊕ ↑(W \ G.J)) → (α ⊕ ↑(W \ G.J)) → Prop}
    (hr : (G.extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes W hW).IsTopologicalOrder r) :
    G.IsTopologicalOrder (fun v w => r (Sum.inl v) (Sum.inl w)) := by

lean_explanation:
This row formalises the LN's `\Rem` immediately after def_3_13 (the intervention-node extension $G_{\doit(I_W)}$) as four named objects: three theorems witnessing the three distinct mathematical statements the LN bundles into a single paragraph, plus a standalone noncomputable relation `extensionOrder` packaging the LN's "put all `I_w` first" recipe so that downstream chapters can quote it by name.

Across all four declarations, `α : Type*` is the ambient vertex type (no `[Finite α]` instance is required) and `G : CDMG α` is the LN's $G = (J, V, E, L)$. `W : Set α` is the LN's $W$ in $I_W$, with `hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` the well-typedness precondition inherited from def_3_13. The extended graph's carrier is the disjoint sum `α ⊕ ↑(W \ G.J)`: `Sum.inl v` is an original node and `Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩` is the LN's fresh $I_w$ for $w \in W \setminus J$. In the extension theorem `r : α → α → Prop` is the LN's topological order on $G$; in the restriction theorem `r` is a topological order on the extended graph (a relation on the bigger carrier).

Mathlib idioms worth flagging: `Sum.inl` / `Sum.inr` are the canonical injections into the disjoint sum (encoding the LN's original/fresh dichotomy at the type level); `↑(W \ G.J)` is the coerced subtype `{ x : α // x ∈ W \ G.J }`, Lean's way of carrying the LN's set of fresh intervention nodes; `Sum.inl_injective` is the underlying-element extraction used to undo a `Sum.inl` lift; `extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_J` / `_V` / `mem_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_E` are the `@[simp]` projection lemmas of def_3_13 that transport node and edge membership across the extension.

The four declarations have these shapes. `isAcyclic_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes` takes `hW` and `h : G.IsAcyclic` and concludes acyclicity of the extended graph. `extensionOrder` is a `noncomputable def` whose body is a four-case pattern match on the `Sum`-carrier: same-side `(Sum.inl, Sum.inl)` and `(Sum.inr, Sum.inr)` cases inherit `r` from the underlying `α`-values; the cross case `(Sum.inr, Sum.inl)` is `True` (encoding "every $I_w$ precedes every original node"); its contrapositive `(Sum.inl, Sum.inr)` is `False`. `isTopologicalOrder_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_extend` takes `hW` and `hr : G.IsTopologicalOrder r` and concludes that `extensionOrder (S := W \ G.J) r` is a topological order of the extended graph (a four-field `IsTopologicalOrder` bundle of `irrefl`, `trans`, `trichotomous`, `parent_lt`). `isTopologicalOrder_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_restrict` takes `hW` and a topological order `r` of the extended graph and concludes that the restriction `fun v w => r (Sum.inl v) (Sum.inl w)` is a topological order of `G`.
design_choices:
**The LN's single `\Rem` is split into three theorems plus one named construction.** Forré--Mooij bundle three distinct mathematical statements under one paragraph: acyclicity preservation, the restriction "a topological order for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is also one for $G$", and the extension "any topological order of $G$ can be extended to one for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$, e.g. by putting all the $I_w$ nodes first". Lean splits them because the three halves have different downstream consumers: claim_3_14 / claim_3_15 (commutation lemmas) need acyclicity *alone* to discharge their CADMG side conditions; the iSCM and do-calculus chapters quote either the extension or the restriction direction in isolation. A bundled conjunction would force every consumer either to project from an `∧` or to drag in topological-order data they do not need. The split is also *structurally forced* here in a way it is not for claim_3_3 (the hard-intervention analogue): `extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes` changes the carrier from `α` to `α ⊕ ↑(W \ G.J)`, so a topological order of $G$ and a topological order of the extended graph live on *different types* and the LN's two clauses are genuinely two transports between distinct relation spaces. This mirrors the two-theorem decomposition of claim_3_6 for `nodeSplittingOn`, which shares the same carrier-change structure.

**`extensionOrder` is a standalone named definition parameterised by `S : Set α`, not `W : Set α` with a `G.J` exclusion hard-coded.** The LN names the recipe via the prose "$I_W$", but the *fresh* node set in def_3_13 is actually $W \setminus J$. Naming the parameter `S` and leaving its identity abstract makes the construction agnostic to whether one is extending by all of $W$, by $W \setminus J$, or by any other subtype carrier, and removes any `G`-dependence from the definition itself (the `G` enters only at the use site of `isTopologicalOrder_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_extend`, which writes `extensionOrder (S := W \ G.J) r` to pin `S` explicitly). This mirrors `splitOrder` (claim_3_6), which is similarly factored as a standalone `noncomputable def` that downstream rows can quote by name.

**Asymmetric `True` / `False` cross cases in `extensionOrder`.** The LN's recipe is asymmetric: every fresh $I_w$ precedes every original node, never the other way around. Encoding the cross case `(Sum.inr, Sum.inl)` as the constant `True` and its contrapositive `(Sum.inl, Sum.inr)` as `False` makes this asymmetry directly visible in the definition and makes both `trichotomous` and `trans` transport trivially on cross pairs (one disjunct of trichotomy is always available; transitivity through a cross step is absorbed into the same-side cases). For the `parent_lt` field, the cross slot is exactly where the fresh edge `Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩ → Sum.inl w` of def_3_13 lands, so `True` discharges the parent-lt obligation for fresh edges with no appeal to the underlying `hr`. Same-side cases inherit `r` from the underlying `α`-values; the choice on `(Sum.inr, Sum.inr)` is free (there are no edges between fresh nodes), but inheriting `r` is the cleanest choice that keeps the relation total / transitive / irreflexive without needing a side relation on `↑S`.

**The restriction direction is included as a Lean-only artefact.** The LN states only the implication "topological order on the extended graph $\Rightarrow$ topological order on $G$", which corresponds to the restriction direction. In the hard-intervention analogue (claim_3_3) the carrier stays `α`, so the same relation `r` serves as a topological order of both graphs and no separate restriction theorem is needed. Here the carrier *changes*, so the LN's prose hides a genuine transport: from a relation on `α ⊕ ↑(W \ G.J)` we must produce a relation on `α`. The natural encoding is `fun v w => r (Sum.inl v) (Sum.inl w)`, restricting `r` along the canonical `Sum.inl` embedding. The restriction direction is named explicitly here so that downstream consumers (claim_3_14 / claim_3_15 commutation; iSCM identification proofs that compute on the CADMG side and want a topological order back on $G$) can quote it without re-deriving the transport each time. This is a non-obvious point a reader coming in from claim_3_3 might otherwise miss.

**`noncomputable` `extensionOrder` and no `[Finite α]` hypothesis on any theorem.** The base relation `r` is `Prop`-valued and need not be decidable; marking `extensionOrder` `noncomputable` keeps the construction consistent with `splitOrder` (claim_3_6) and with `extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes` itself, and downstream uses are all `Prop`-valued so the classical choice has no observable cost. Neither the acyclicity theorem (proved by a direct walk-lifting argument) nor the two topological-order theorems (proved by field-by-field transport via the `@[simp]` membership lemmas of def_3_13) requires finiteness. Downstream callers in iSCM chapters working over not-yet-finitised vertex types inherit this directly.
